asset_type: Rail scheme (new station, line upgrade, electrification)
description: New rail station, line reopening, electrification, or capacity enhancement. Appraised using DfT TAG with rail-specific modules.

new_station.note: Simple halt to major interchange. Excludes land. Reference: Beeching reopenings programme.

line_reopening_per_km.note: Depends heavily on whether trackbed is intact, structures condition, signalling scope.

electrification_per_km.note: OLE. Wide range depending on bridge clearances, re-gauging, power supply.

capacity_enhancement.note: Platform lengthening at the low end, major junction remodelling at the high end.

typical_benefits.monetisation: DfT TAG values of time (work: ~GBP 20/hr, commuting: ~GBP 7/hr)
DfT TAG crowding multipliers applied to time savings

typical_benefits.description: Passengers switching from car to rail
typical_benefits.monetisation: Decongestion benefits, accident reduction, carbon savings

typical_benefits.description: Journeys that would not otherwise occur (generated demand)
typical_benefits.monetisation: Consumer surplus from rule of half
Present separately from core BCR. Venables (2007), Graham (2007).

typical_benefits.description: Net carbon impact (electrification reduces operational emissions; construction has embodied carbon)
Property values increase near new stations
typical_benefits.monetisation: Do NOT include alongside journey time savings (double counting risk). Present as supplementary only.

default_parameters.optimism_bias_note: Rail projects have the highest optimism bias of any project type. Flyvbjerg (2003) found average cost overrun of 45% for rail.
default_parameters.benefit_ramp_up_note: Rail demand builds gradually. TAG assumes full forecast demand reached within 3-7 years of opening.

reference_bcr_range.note: Rail BCRs are highly variable. Small stations on existing lines can have high BCRs. Major infrastructure (tunnels, viaducts) often has BCRs below 1.0 and proceeds on strategic/connectivity grounds.
reference_cases: HS2 Phase 1 FBC (2020): BCR 1.2 (including WEIs), 0.9 (core BCR only)
Beeching reopenings programme (2020): indicative BCRs ranged 0.4-3.5
Northern Powerhouse Rail SOC (2019): BCR 1.0-2.5 depending on option
Typical small station: BCR 2-4 (low cost, concentrated time savings)

typical_risks: Cost overruns: rail has the highest average overrun of any project type (Flyvbjerg 2003: 45%)
Demand forecasting error: Flyvbjerg (2005) found rail demand overestimated by 50% on average
Network Rail possession availability (construction windows constrained by timetable)
Signalling system complexity (integration with existing network)
Political risk (long planning/construction timelines span multiple administrations)
Electrification: bridge clearance issues, power supply constraints

publications: Green Book Supplementary Guidance: Optimism Bias
reference_evaluations: DfT POPE (Post Opening Project Evaluation) for rail
What Works Centre: Transport Evidence Review (2015)
